coredata.dc:title: Hydrophobic and anti-fouling novel anti-corrosion coatings of graphene quantum dots in situ doped with polyphenylene sulfide
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Li
Nian, Fang
Zhang, Shenggui
Xu, Yumei
Ma, Sudai
Li, Yongjuan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots have a promising application in the frontier field due to their small size and high dispersion. In this paper, graphene quantum dots were prepared by top-down oxide cutting method, and stable composite coatings were prepared by doping in polyphenylene sulfide with different ratios. The results show that the composite coating containing 5 % GQDs/PPS has the largest water contact angle and good hydrophobicity, and the weight loss rate is only 23.37 % at 800 °C, which proves its good thermal stability. Electrochemical tests showed that the corrosion potential of the 5 % GQDs/PPS composite coating reached −323 mV, and the corrosion current was 5.45 × 10−10 A/cm2 . The results of corrosion resistance experiments showed that the 5 % GQDs/PPS composite coating had the best corrosion resistance in H2SO4, NaOH and NaCl solutions. This study reveals that graphene quantum dots can prepare hydrophobic anticorrosive coatings with excellent performance and bring convenience for the protection of substrates.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots have a promising application in the frontier field due to their small size and high dispersion. In this paper, graphene quantum dots were prepared by top-down oxide cutting method, and stable composite coatings were prepared by doping in polyphenylene sulfide with different ratios. The results show that the composite coating containing 5 % GQDs/PPS has the largest water contact angle and good hydrophobicity, and the weight loss rate is only 23.37 % at 800 °C, which proves its good thermal stability. Electrochemical tests showed that the corrosion potential of the 5 % GQDs/PPS composite coating reached −323 mV, and the corrosion current was 5.45 × 10−10 A/cm2 . The results of corrosion resistance experiments showed that the 5 % GQDs/PPS composite coating had the best corrosion resistance in H2SO4, NaOH and NaCl solutions. This study reveals that graphene quantum dots can prepare hydrophobic anticorrosive coatings with excellent performance and bring convenience for the protection of substrates.

ce:section.ce:para: Raw lacquer, which comes from the tropical lacquer tree, is a renewable material and is considered one of the best natural protective coatings due to its excellent barrier to oxygen and water, as well as its excellent solvent resistance and physical and mechanical properties for long-term preservation [1–4]. However, it also has many disadvantages, such as slow drying and poor practicality of raw lacquer, which have become obstacles in its application. Moreover, lacquer trees that have grown for more than ten years will mature, and the slow-growing lacquer trees and the fast-growing industrial demand create a situation where the supply exceeds the demand. Therefore, the development of new industrial coatings has been a subject of interest for researchers. In recent years, polyphenylene sulfide (PPS) has been used as a special engineering material in various fields because of its excellent chemical resistance, mechanical properties, good adhesion, and excellent high-temperature fluidity. PPS also forms good compatibility with many additive polymers, so that polyphenylene sulfide can be better processed and modified. For example, Wu et al. prepared a PPS/PTFE-bronze coating by electrohydrodynamic jet deposition to improve the friction properties and corrosion resistance of steel. The results showed that the coating had good lubricity and corrosion resistance [5]. Li et al. synthesized a novel benzoxazine monomer (BTE) containing triazole and Easter functional groups and coated it in PPS fabrics, demonstrating strong antimicrobial and flame-retardant properties, as well as improved mechanical properties and acid resistance [6]. PPS is a new type of functional engineering plastic. It is a polymer containing p-phenylene sulfide repeating structural units in the molecule and is widely used in electronics, automotive, aerospace, mechanical, and chemical industries [7–10]. The polyphenylene sulfide used in this paper was synthesized from N, N-2-methylpyrrolidone with sodium sulfide ninhydrate and dichlorobenzene by SN2 nucleophilic substitution reaction [11]. Its microscopic morphology has flower-like, spherical, and sheet-like structures with large and controllable size spans and two types of structures, hyperbranched and purely linear, spanning the typical polymerization degree of chemical materials in general. Polyphenylene sulfide is known as a new non-traditional “all-purpose material” with many advantages: mechanical dimensional stability, high insulating properties, high toughness, solvent resistance, high temperature resistance, etc. [5,12–14], but they have not been widely used because of their stable properties and difficulty in handling [15,16]. Fortunately, polyphenylene sulfide can also be used directly as a catalyst or matrix for composites and can be compounded with carbon-based materials with specific functions to form high-quality composites for use in coatings with anti-corrosion properties.

In recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have become a material that can be incorporated into high-performance polymers [2,17] due to their good water solubility, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, strong bioaffinity, rich surface activity, and easy functionalization, which can improve the performance of composite materials. Its precursor graphite oxide is widely used in anti-corrosion coatings, such as barrier properties of composites to liquids and gases [18,19], excellent chemical stability and heat resistance [20,21], remarkable mechanical rigidity and strength, corrosion resistance, microbial resistance, and anti-pollution [22]. Therefore, using graphene quantum dots as anti-corrosion fillers is feasible to improve polymer coatings and conventional organic coating formulations. This allows the preparation of new graphene quantum dots and other matrix composites to be used for corrosion protection of valuable equipment in plants and to improve the mechanical properties of other polymers [21,23]. Although graphite oxide (GO) has been used to prepare protective coatings, its dispersion has become an open problem due to its tendency to agglomerate. Graphene quantum dots can be completely dissolved in a variety of solvents with uniform dispersion and less agglomeration, which can solve this problem well. In addition, due to the self-assembly property of graphene quantum dots, the reaction with other polymers can also promote and drive their own assembly and stack into nanostructures of different sizes [24,25], thus preparing to obtain graphene oxide nanoparticles (Nano-GO) with multi-scale structures. Nano-graphene oxide particles can be tightly doped and stacked in the surface and pores of polyphenylene sulfide [26,27], which not only solves the problem of large layers, stacking, and aggregation of graphene oxide but also overcomes the drawback of poor compatibility with polymers [28–32].

In this study, the preparation and properties of graphene quantum dot in-situ doped polyphenylene sulfide composite coatings were investigated, and the effects of the content of graphene quantum dots on the morphology, crystallinity, chemical bonding, and thermal stability of GQDs/PPS composites were discussed. In addition, the excellent corrosion resistance of the material was demonstrated by testing the impedance and polarization curves and acid, alkali, and salt immersion corrosion tests. Finally, the anti-corrosion mechanism of the GQDs/PPS composite coating was explained.

Sodium sulfide hydrate (Na2S·9H2O), N, N-dimethyl pyrrolidone (NMP), Lithium chloride (LiCl), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), para-Dichlorobenzene, Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), Hydrogen peroxide(H2O2), all chemicals used in the experiment were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The experiment added 79.5 g of sodium sulfide hydrate to 200 mL of NMP solution. Since sodium sulfide is very hygroscopic, nitrogen was continuously poured into a three-necked flask to remove oxygen from the flask. The solution was warmed slowly and 44.1 g of p-dichlorobenzene was added with stirring; the reactor was sealed and the stirring speed was set to 900 r/min with continuous stirring. After the reaction was completed and warmed up to room temperature, the solution was diluted with distilled water in a ratio of 1:1 to obtain a turbid solution with a white precipitate; the solution was washed several times with a mixture of deionized water and ethanol, and then filtered to obtain a flower-like PPS (Fig. 1 ).

Weigh 0.1 g of 3D network graphene in 120 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid, and stir it magnetically at 5 °C for 10 min, then slowly add 0.8 g of KMnO4, heat it to 150 °C, and stir it at a constant temperature for 24 h. At the end of the reaction, the graphene was oxidized and cut into graphene quantum dots, and then pour the reaction solution into 5 °C of iced water with rapid stirring, and then add 3 mL of H2O2 dropwise after it cooled down to room temperature to obtain a bright yellow graphene quantum dot solution. After cooling to room temperature, 3 mL of H2O2 was added dropwise to obtain a bright yellow graphene quantum dot solution. The bright yellow graphene quantum dot solution was obtained. After disassembling the condensation device, the liquid was evaporated at 85 °C with continuous stirring, and after the device was completely cooled down, a light brown graphene quantum dot solid was obtained. The obtained graphene quantum dots dispersion emitted green fluorescence under the irradiation of a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm and the yield was initially estimated to be about 75 %.

The floral polyphenylene sulfide and graphene quantum dots were mixed in the ratios of 99.5:0.5, 99:1, 98:2, 96:4, and 94:6, respectively, and added to the organic solvent NMP, and the reaction was stirred at 220 °C for 4 h and 270–275 °C for 2 h to obtain the grey precipitated material; the material was lowered to room temperature and filtered, washed repeatedly with ethanol and acetone, and freeze-dried to obtain. The graphene quantum dot self-assembled composites doped with flower-like polyphenylene sulfide (Fig. 2 ).

The composite coating is applied under the international standard method. Mix the composite material with the film-forming agent by solution blending and stirring at room temperature, apply the composite coating on a clean and dry tinplate, cure at room temperature for 24 h, soak at 100 °C for 5 min, and then dry at 120 °C after 2.5 h, dry test after the film is fixed and then proceed to the next experiment. The characterization items are: the coating on the iron sheet is used for physical tests such as thickness, adhesion, and hardness, the sample size is 10 × 50 × 0.1 mm, the drying time is counted, and the chemical resistance test is tested [33,34]. In addition, pure PPS coating film and GQDs coating film were also tested as control samples.

Select mica powder, cobalt hydroxide, and nano polymer to add the appropriate amount of waterborne epoxy acrylate and solvent. The remaining acrylic resin and solvent are to be used subsequently to avoid adding them at one time and causing the solvent to play too fast.
Add the above-selected ingredients in turn and disperse them in high-speed stirring until there are no obvious particles in the modulated slurry to make a homogeneous and stable resin slurry.

Add the above-mentioned prepared mixed resin solution to the appropriate amount of curing agent, solvent, and additives in turn, and put them into the high-speed mixer until the mixture is uniform and adjust the viscosity.
Select tinplate as the test plate [50 mm × 100 mm × (0.2–0.4 mm)], which needs to be sanded with 400 mesh sandpaper to remove the surface oxide film before spraying.

Spray the plate using high-pressure air spray (pressure 0.022 MPa, flow rate 380 mL/min) spraying method, adjust the air pressure and gun nozzle, move the gun smoothly, and control the distance between the gun and the substrate at about 20 cm to finally get a complete, uniform and wetted coating. Spray three times: the first time to cover the pretreated substrate; the second time to establish the anti-corrosion coating system; and the third time to increase the thickness of the coating and to level the surface.
After the surface of the paint film is dry, put it in a 60 °C oven to cure for 6 h or put it at room temperature for a week, and take it out to dry to test the performance.

To further confirm the elemental composition of the prepared graphene quantum dots, we performed XPS characterization, Fig. 3 shows the full XPS spectrum and fitted splitting peaks of the graphene quantum dots, the XPS spectra were performed by Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB 250Xi (Mono Al Ka, 1486.6 eV, 16 kV, 15 mA, 2 × 10−9 m bar, CAE Scan Mode) was performed. Fig. 3A shows that the graphene quantum dots consist of C, O, and N elements with atomic concentrations of 65.13 at.%, 31.73 at.%, and 3.14 at.%, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of C1s in Fig. 3B show that the absorption peaks of CC, CC, CN, CO and CO bonds in the structure of graphene quantum dots are located at 284.7, 285.2, 285.4, 286.1, and 289.1 eV of the binding energy, respectively, and the presence of the CC bond indicates that there is a benzene ring structure in the graphene quantum dots, and the existence of the CO and CO bonds indicates that the graphene quantum dots have been prepared during the preparation process. The presence of CO and CO bonds indicates the presence of oxidation during the preparation of the quantum dots, forming oxygen-containing functional groups or oxygen defects [35]. The high-resolution spectra of O1s with binding energies at 531.5, 532.5, and 533.9 eV in Fig. 3C are attributed to OH, CO, and COC, respectively [36], C and O binding forms on graphene quantum dots are demonstrated. The high-resolution spectra of N1s can be seen in Fig. 3D, with binding energies at 399.82, 400.6, 401.3, and 402.1 eV corresponding to pyridine N, pyrrolidine N, graphite N, and nitrogen oxide (N+), respectively, because the doped form of nitrogen is also present in graphene quantum dots [37], Consistent with the infrared spectroscopy results, which may be due to the introduction of nitrogen-containing reactants during the experimental process, the curve fitting further determined the surface phase composition of graphene quantum dots, proving the successful preparation of graphene quantum dots [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots with particle size and zeta potential
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4A shows the histogram of particle size distribution and Zeta potential for graphene quantum dots, which is the potential at an imaginary sliding interface near the outer layer of the double layer on the surface of a material in solution, and can approximate the electrostatic charge potential carried by the surface of a substance in a liquid (generally aqueous solution), correlated with the stability of the gellar dispersion. In our experiments, we observed that graphene quantum dots have hyperdispersion in aqueous solutions and graphene quantum dots have an obvious Tyndall effect, so it is necessary to characterize the electrostatic charge potential on its surface by the zeta potential. Fig. 4B shows the particle size distribution of graphene quantum dots with a wide range of distribution, concentrated around 1.5 nm, whose zeta potential value is −0.89 mV, The zeta potentials are all negative, indicating that the surfaces of graphene quantum dots are all negatively charged.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomic force microscopy of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: To further analyze the morphology and size of graphene quantum dots, the sample was characterized by AFM, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5A shows the graphene quantum dots in a uniformly dispersed state, and Fig. 5B shows a three-dimensional view of the graphene quantum dots, which can visualize the distribution situation of graphene quantum dots, and the combination of the two figures can easily understand the planar distribution of graphene quantum dots. Fig. 5C shows the cross-sectional height of the graphene quantum dots in the cross-sectional part of the atomic force microscope diagram. From the analysis data of the cross-sectional height, the height of the graphene quantum dots obtained in the cross-section here is about 5 nm, and since the cross-sectional lines in the plane are random the graphene quantum dots are distributed more uniformly in the diagram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and XRD characterization of GQDs/PPS materials
ce:section.ce:para: The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) detects the reaction mechanism of the sample by the KBr particle method. The method operates in the range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 with a scan rate of 0.2 cm/s and a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1. Fig. 6A shows the FT-IR spectrum, a C-O-C vibrational absorption peak appears at 1203 cm−1, which is consistent with the absorption peak of epoxy groups on GQDs [39]. They are caused by the asymmetric stretching vibration of CH, which is caused by the carbon in GQDs connecting two hydrogens; the absorption peak at 1710 cm−1 belongs to the absorption peak of CO on the carbonyl group; 2816 cm−1 The appearing broad weak peak belongs to the peak of sp3 hybridized C-H; 3434 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of the associated OH in the sample. It can be seen from the infrared spectrum that the characteristic absorption peaks appear in the characteristic functional groups, which indicates the successful preparation of the obtained GQDs. Curve b in Fig. 6A, the samples have vibration absorption peaks of benzene skeleton at 1584 cm−1, 1490 cm−1, and 1390 cm−1, CS vibration of aromatic carbon at 1090 cm−1 [40], the weak peaks at 1905 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 are the flexion vibration peaks and the combined frequency peaks of the CH bond on the substituted benzene; the CH out-of-plane vibration of 818 cm−1 para-substituted benzene ring. It can be seen from the infrared spectrum that the characteristic absorption peaks appear in the characteristic functional groups, which indicates the successful preparation of the obtained PPS. In Fig. 6A (c), all the characteristic peaks of polyphenylene sulfide and quantum dots exist in the sample, and the positions of the peaks have not changed. Some peaks at 3000 cm−1 become obvious, indicating that the double bond carbon amount of hydrogen has increased, which also proves the increase in the number and proportion of benzene rings.

Fig. 6B shows the XRD patterns of GQDs composites with different contents, tested under Cu Ka, λ = 1.5418 Å, Lynx 1D array detector, −110 to 168°, and Bruker AXS D8-Advance diffractometer with standard configuration software to analyze the crystalline phases pure PPS and GQDs/PPS. From Fig. 6B (b), it can be seen that the characteristic peaks of the graphene (002) crystal plane appear at the diffraction angle of 10.5°; the diffraction peak at 22.0° indicates that the graphene features are more obvious on the planes with smaller scales of GQDs [41]. Fig. 6B (a) shows the diffraction peaks of poly(phenylene sulfide) at 17.5°–22.5°, and the characteristic peaks of poly(phenylene sulfide) appeared on the (110), (111), (112), and (211) crystal planes [42]. Among them, the diffraction peak on the crystal surface of (111) at 20.5° can be proved to be polyphenylene sulfide. Comparing the crystalline peaks of pure PPS support and GQDs/PPS composites, no new crystalline peaks were found on the XRD patterns of the composite coatings, and the characteristic peaks of GQDs did not disappear. Therefore, the GQD particles existed on the petal surface of PPS with a bouquet-like structure. No impurity peaks were observed, which proved that the purity of GQDs/PPS composites was high.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to determine the surface elemental composition, chemical state and molecular structure of GQDs/PPS composite samples, ESCALAB 250Xi (Mono Al Ka, 1486.6 eV, 16 kV, 15 mA, 2 × 10−9 m bar, CAE Scan Mode). Fig. 7D shows the full spectrum including the characteristic peaks of O1s, C1s, and S2p. As shown in Fig. 7B, the C1s spectrum has six diffraction peaks including CS, CC/C/CC, CO, CO/COS, COC, and O-CO. Fig. 7C is the spectrum of O1s with O-CO, CO, and COC/OH. There are three characteristic signal peaks [25]. Fig. 7C shows the S2p spectrum that the silicon atoms have two chemical environments, the SC and the OS characteristic peaks, which correspond to the FTIR results. This result confirms that the chemical bonding form of GQDs and PPS molecular composites has worked. It is also clear that the GQDs have fully entered between the two interfaces of these PPS polymer molecules. These results are also in agreement with previous analyses.

Thermal fields emission scanning electron microscope showing the morphology of GO, GQDs, pure PPS, GQDs/PPS composites, the morphologies of samples were obtained using Zeiss Ultra Plus Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM, 0.02 kV–30 kV, Germany). The surface morphology of the GO composite films in Fig. 8A has obvious protrusions and agglomeration effects [43]. Demonstrates GO film thickness and good dispersibility. Comparing Fig. 8B and A, graphene quantum dots have good dispersibility. Pure graphene quantum dots have self-assembly effects in the report [44], but their crystal lattices are very obvious, showing a regular hexagon. Fig. 8C is an electron micrograph of pure polyphenylene sulfide. The self-assembled sheet-like layered structure of the polyphenylene sulfide shows the thickness and large specific surface area of the PPS sheet and also provides a large number of doping sites. Fig. 8D is a scanning electron microscope image of GQDs/PPS composite material at low magnification. In this figure, we can see that GQDs/PPS composite material has very good dispersibility. Fig. 8E is an electron microscope image of the GQDs/PPS composite, it can be seen from the figure that the laminar voids of the composite structure become smaller. This change can make the composite material better resistant to corrosive agents and protect industrial equipment from corrosion by acids, alkalis, and salts. At the same time, some negative reunions have also occurred [45]. This phenomenon is strongly related to the strong interfacial interaction of the polymer matrix, and the gaps between the sheets are reduced, in the enlarged Fig. 8F, it can be seen that the surface of the polyphenylene sulfide is self-assembled to uniformly distribute the GQDs layer, which proves that the composite was successfully synthesized from the microstructure. This result is also consistent with the FT-IR analysis result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of heat resistance of coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9A shows the thermal weight loss curves of PPS, GQDs, and GQDs/PPS composites under nitrogen protection, which shows the thermal decomposition temperature of each component. When the temperature reaches 800 °C, the final weight loss is 35.60 % for PPS and 58.70 % for GQDs. The expected result is that the final weight loss of the GQDs/PPS composite is only 23.37 %, indicating the good thermal stability of the GQDs/PPS composite coating. It can be observed from the figure that the initial thermal decomposition temperature of GQDs/PPS composite coating is much higher than that of GQDs, which is due to the addition of graphene quantum dots filling the voids of the polyphenylene sulfide material, especially when the doping mass is 5 %, the void spacing is the smallest, making the best stability and the smallest thermal weight loss. The experimental results show that GQDs and PPS materials can improve the thermal stability of GQDs/PPS composites through doping interactions. Fig. 9B shows the DSC curves of pure PPS, 5 % GQDs/PPS, and GQDs. It is noteworthy that the melting peak of the composites was improved compared with pure PPS and GQDs, which can be used as a future application of heat-resistant materials.

Fig. 10 shows the SEM images of the coating surface prepared by different materials, where Fig. 10A–E show the surface morphology of the coating with different compositions and ratios, respectively, and it can be observed that with the regulation of the components and ratios, the coating surface changes significantly, and the changing trend is closely related to the content of GQDs and PPS, the higher the content of PPS, the rougher the coating surface, and with the change of the GQD doping amount, the coating surface changes from With the change of GQD doping amount, the coating surface gradually becomes flat from rough and porous. Moreover, from the microscopic images of the coating, there is no obvious agglomeration of the material in the coating, while it can be seen from the breakage of the coating (E) containing 5 % GQDs/PPS material that the coating surface is formed by the interlocking mosaic of the lamellae, which is a mutual confirmation of the flower-like structure formed by the in situ doping of GQDs in the PPS lamellae structure during the formation of the material. The cross-sectional morphology of the coating is observed in Fig. 10F. By measuring different locations with a scale and taking the average value, the coating thickness is about 63 μm, which is basically consistent with the construction thickness of anti-corrosion coatings in industrial production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wetting performance analysis of the coating
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophobic angle of a droplet on the surface of a sample was measured using a contact angle meter model SL200KB. The volume of the measured droplets was 3 μL The average of the measured values at five different locations was taken as the final hydrophobic angle of the sample. Fig. 11 shows the results of the wettability tests of the coatings prepared from different materials, from which the variation of the water contact angle can be seen. Pure GQDs and pure PPS were used as raw materials to prepare coatings with smaller angles, 73° and 78°, respectively. When different percentages of doped GQDs/PPS were added, the water contact angle became significantly larger, and the coating containing 5 % GQDs/PPS material had the largest angle, which could reach 92°, indicating that the coating had good hydrophobicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical testing of GQDs/PPS composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: A three-electrode system with a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum electrode as the counter electrode and a sample as the working electrode was used to test the corrosion resistance of the composite coatings. Fig. 12 shows the electrochemical test results of GQDs/PPS composite coatings with different GQDs content. The test solution is a 3.5 % NaCl solution, and the test items are polarization curve, corrosion potential, and corrosion current. The potentiodynamic polarization curves (Tafel) are conducted to investigate the instantaneous corrosion rate of samples. In general, a lower value of corrosion current density (Icorr) indicates a lower dynamic rate of corrosion [46]. As shown in Fig. 12A, the data obtained is analyzed as follows. When containing 5.0 wt% of GQDs, the corrosion potential of the GQDs/PPS composite coating is relatively high, the value is Ec = −323 mV, and the corrosion current is low, the value is Ic = 5.45 × 10−10 A/cm2, indicating that the addition of GQDs significantly improves the corrosion resistance of the GQDs/PPS composite coating, which indicates that the addition of GQDs (5.0 %) composite coating has the best corrosion resistance.

In this study, the GQDs content of 7 % has a significant influence on the corrosion rate of the GQDs/PPS composite coating. The sample shows the lowest Ic = 1.326 × 10−9 A/cm2 and the corrosion rate is comparable. The reason for the previous decrease is that the agglomeration effect of GQDs leads to poor film-forming ability, and the uneven distribution also reduces the adhesion to metals and other substrates and reduces the protection.

Fig. 12B, C shows the Nyquist and Bode plots of the GQDs/PPS composite coating, tested by an electrochemical workstation. In the electrolyte was a 3.5 % NaCl solution to further investigate the effect of different GQDs content on the performance of the GQDs/PPS composite coating. In general, a larger semicircle diameter in Nyquist plots (charge transfer resistance) means a lower corrosion rate [47]. In the Nyquist plot, the GQDs/PPS composite coating samples were characterized at high voltage and low current. The larger the arc resistance radius, the better the corrosion resistance of the coating, the more difficult the electron transfer, the worse the conductivity, and the higher the impedance. In this experiment, it can be seen from the Nyquist plot that when the doping amount is 3.0 %, the impedance modulus is 1.98 × 105 Ω∙cm2, which is improved compared with the corrosion performance of pure GQDs, proving that the doping is necessary, and when the doping amount continues to increase, the capacitive arc of the GQDs/PPS composite sample is the largest and the resistance is the larger when the GQDs are 5.0 %, and the impedance modulus is about 7.2 × 105 Ω∙cm2, which indicates that the addition of graphene quantum dots not only did not exhibit electrical conductivity but also greatly improved the corrosion resistance of the coated samples. The experimental results and the results of thermal weight loss analysis also proved that the additional amount of GQDs had a great influence on the properties of the composites.

In Bode plots, the impedance modulus at the lowest measured frequency (|Z|0.1 Hz) was often applied as a semi-quantitative indicator to estimate the shielding function of the coating [48]. When the doping amount continues to increase to 7.0 %, it can be seen from the graph that the capacitive arc decreases and the impedance modulus decreases to 2.25 × 105 Ω∙cm2, but compared to, pure GQDs, the impedance modulus is greatly improved, which proves that the corrosion resistance of the GQDs/PPS composite coating is improved under this doping condition. It can be concluded that the impedance is maximum when the doping amount is 5 %–7 % of GQDs, and the corresponding corrosion current density is the lowest in the experimental data of the tested polarization curves. It shows that the preparation of the composite and the coating experiments of the iron plate are very successful, especially since the 5.0 % GQDs composite coating has a tighter bouquet structure and the small void structure of the composite can effectively prevent the penetration of corrosion ions (acid, alkali, and salt). The results show that the 5.0 wt% GQDs composite coating has high corrosion resistance. EIS electrochemical simulations were performed using Zview2 software, as shown in Fig. 12D, with the solution transfer resistance Rs, the resistance and capacitance provided by the oxide layer (denoted by Roxide), the charge transfer resistance Rct and the double-layer capacitance Qdl, the coating capacitance Qcoat and the coating resistance Rcoat, and the Wahlberg impedance W (which denotes the diffusion-controlled corrosion process, and which reveals the properties and performance of the coating). A high Rct value indicates a low corrosion rate and good protection, and the Rct value of the 5.0 wt% GQDs composite coating is much higher than that of the other composite coatings, which proves that it can effectively prevent the charge transfer between the substrate and the electrolyte, and has good corrosion resistance. The Cdl values of the composite coatings were reduced by about an order of magnitude at 5.0 wt% GQDs compared to pure GQDs, indicating that the relatively small surface area of corrosion provides good protection [49] (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion resistance of GQDs/PPS composite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the corrosion resistance of GQDs/PPS composite coatings, three commonly used solutions, i.e., 30 % dilute sulfuric acid, 10 % sodium hydroxide solution and 3.5 % sodium chloride solution were used for the chemical corrosion resistance tests. The experimental procedure was as follows: the iron plate with composite coating was immersed in H2SO4, NaOH and NaCl solutions for 1, 3, 5, 7 and 12 h respectively at room temperature, and the experimental phenomena (whether the surface was discolored or blistered) were observed once at each time node. Comparing the iron pieces with different coatings on the surface after immersion, it can be observed that no matter the corrosion degree of 5 % GQDs/PPS composite coatings in sulfuric acid, alkali and salt solutions is very low, there is no obvious corrosion phenomenon, which proves that 5 % GQDs/PPS composite coatings have good stability under the harsh conditions, only the surface of the coatings consisting of PPS and GQDs has a large number of loose corrosion products, which is also from the side of the revelation. The coating composed of PPS and GQDs is unstable (Table 2). The comparison reveals that the polyphenylene sulfide composite coating doped with 5 % GQDs has good corrosion resistance, mainly because when small-sized GQDs are doped in polyphenylene sulfide in situ, they can fill up the gaps between the flower-like polyphenylene sulfide layers through self-assembly, thus blocking the entry of corrosion factors and improving the corrosion resistance of 5 % GQDs/PPS composite coatings.

Comparative photographs (e.g., Fig. 13 ) of different samples after immersion in 3.5 % NaCl solution for several days were selected for visual comparison. It can be seen that the uncoated tin corrodes severely and the PPS coated tin surface only begins to blister, while the surface of the composite plating doped with 5 % GQDs remains unchanged with excellent corrosion resistance after 10, 15 and 20 days of immersion in 3.5 % salt solution.

This experiment can directly prove the corrosion resistance of the composite coating, the protection principle is through the small size of GQDs added in polyphenylene sulfide in the form of in situ self-assembly directly on the surface of the sheet or fill in the gaps between the layers of bouquet polyphenylene sulfide, thus hindering the diffusion of corrosive ions (acid and alkali salts), which enhances the corrosion resistance of the GQDs/PPS composite coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-fouling performance of the coating surface
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 14A shows the anti-fouling performance of the coating was tested using a watercolor pen as a characteristic contaminant. Specifically, contamination marks were left on cardboard, wood, iron plates, and walls, and then water was sprayed over the contaminants to observe the state of the contaminants and water droplets to judge the hydrophobic and anti-fouling performance of the illustrated coating surface. From the figure, it can be seen that the coating behaves obviously hydrophobic and the water is all present in the state of droplets without wetting, which is consistent with the test results of Fig. 11. At the same time, it can be seen that the contaminants gradually detach from the surface of the coating with the water droplets, indicating that the coating also has a detaching effect on the contaminants, with the most obvious surface detachment of the iron sheet. Fig. 14B shows the analysis of the cleaned coating surface, and all samples have good anti-fouling properties with a cleaning efficiency of more than 90 %. In addition, the 5 % GQDs/PPS composite coating was immersed in Congo red and turbid water for antifouling testing, where the friction of the solution on the coating was increased by stirring the test solution with a glass rod before testing. The coating was then immersed in the solution and removed after 10s. As shown in Fig. 14C and D, the surface was free of any residue of the solution components and remained as clean as at the beginning. This set of experiments proved that the more regular micro-nano structure of the coating surface caused the formation of both hydrophobic and oleophobic surfaces of the coating, which can effectively reduce the contact and adhesion of pollutants on the coating surface, resulting in the unsound adhesion relationship between the coating surface and pollutants, and largely improve the hydrophobic and anti-fouling properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the performance of the present work coating with that of coatings reported in the literature
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, many new composite coatings have also been developed for corrosion protection, and in order to compare more intuitively the performance differences between the present work and the coatings that have been reported, we illustrate the advantages and disadvantages between the coatings by means of a Table 3 .

It is well known that metals corrode in the atmosphere in addition to aqueous media [55–57], and that rain, dew and fog can cause corrosion [58]. As illustrated in Fig. 15A, the surface of pure PPS is not repulsive to water and contaminants and may have a large pore structure, corrosive media can easily penetrate the surface of the substrate to undergo a redox reaction, generating a large number of corrosion products at the interface between the coating and the substrate, which results in corrosion of the metal and the loss of its protection. The GQDs, although of a smaller size, still have a graphene structure, which gives them some of the characteristics of the nanoscale GQDs, although small in size, still have a graphene structure, which gives them some of the characteristics of graphene, a nano-scale material. When doped in situ in the middle of the PPS, the carbon ring structure of the GQDs and the stability of the PPS produce a synergistic effect, resulting in the formation of a dense, crack-free surface, which is a good way to fill the defects on the surface of the pure PPS, thus providing an excellent barrier performance. It prevents the formation of pitting corrosion, provides corrosion protection in the vertical direction of the coating thickness, and prevents the diffusion of the corrosion path, thus delaying or stopping the corrosion (Fig. 15B), thus achieving the purpose of corrosion prevention.

GQDs/PPS composites were successfully prepared by in-situ doping of GQDs into PPS polymers using the solution mixing method. It was found that the in situ doping of graphene quantum dots in polyphenylene sulfide could fill the interlayer voids of polyphenylene sulfide and co-assemble with polyphenylene sulfide, which enhanced the bonding between GQDs and PPS composites. The experimental results showed that the best corrosion resistance of the coating was obtained when the addition amount of GQDs was 5.0 wt%, which was attributed to the synergistic effect of GQDs doped in this composition with polymer PPS to obtain a hydrophobic and antifouling coating surface, to reduce the ionic channels on the surface of the substrate, to prevent the invasion of corrosive media such as corrosive elements acid, alkali, salt, etc., and to obtain a good corrosion protection effect. In conclusion, the GQDs/PPS composite coatings prepared in this work provide a feasible strategy for anti-corrosion studies.
